title: Reading a stem-and-leaf plot
problem:
The stem-and-leaf plot shows the ages (in years) of $9$ people at a sports club.

$$\begin{array}{r|l} \text{Stem} & \text{Leaf} \\ \hline 1 & 5 \quad 8 \quad 8 \\ 2 & 0 \quad 3 \quad 7 \\ 3 & 1 \quad 4 \quad 9 \end{array}$$

Key: $1 \mid 5$ means $15$.

Find (a) the mode and (b) the median.

steps.instruction: Read the plot to list all values in order. The stem gives the tens digit and each leaf gives the units digit.
Find the mode by identifying the value that appears most often.
steps.working: $18$ appears twice; all other values appear once. Mode $= 18$.

steps.instruction: Find the median. With $9$ values, the median is the $5$th value in the ordered list.

title: Reading a histogram
problem:
The table below summarises a histogram showing the number of hours $40$ students spent studying in one week.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline \text{Hours} & \text{Frequency} \\ \hline [0, 2) & 4 \\ [2, 4) & 8 \\ [4, 6) & 12 \\ [6, 8) & 10 \\ [8, 10) & 6 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Find (a) the modal class, (b) the relative frequency of the $[4, 6)$ class, and (c) the number of students who studied for fewer than $4$ hours.

steps.instruction: Find the modal class by identifying the interval with the highest frequency.
steps.working: Frequencies: $4, \ 8, \ 12, \ 10, \ 6$. The highest is $12$, in the class $[4, 6)$. Modal class $= [4, 6)$.

steps.instruction: Calculate the relative frequency of $[4, 6)$ by dividing its frequency by the total.
Add the frequencies for all classes below $4$ hours.

title: Choosing the right statistical display
problem:
For each situation below, identify the most appropriate statistical display and give a reason.

(a) A teacher records the individual test scores (out of $50$) for $30$ students.

(b) A scientist measures the heights (in cm) of $1500$ seedlings, ranging from $5$ cm to $85$ cm.

(c) A shop records its daily revenue every day for two years.

steps.instruction: For (a): identify the data type and quantity, then match to an appropriate display.
steps.working: The data is numerical with $30$ individual values. A stem-and-leaf plot groups values by tens digit, preserves each individual score, and clearly shows the shape of the distribution for a moderate-sized dataset.

steps.instruction: For (b): consider the large number of values and wide range.
steps.working: With $1500$ values spanning $5$ to $85$ cm, a histogram groups values into class intervals. This makes the distribution readable without the overcrowding that a dot plot or stem-and-leaf would produce.

steps.instruction: For (c): consider that the data is recorded over a sequence of time periods.
steps.working: Daily revenue over two years is a time-series dataset. A line graph connects consecutive data points, making trends, seasonal patterns and unusual days easy to identify.
